figures.caption: Figure 1: Three-prompt design used throughout the paper. Prompt A is the neutral baseline, Prompt B adds an explicit cultural instruction, and Prompt C adds only implicit situational cueing. PCS asks how much of the Prompt A→B shift is recovered in Prompt A→C.

Figure 4: Feature-level implicit sensitivity using median stable PCS across languages. Authority-related features dominate the top of the ranking. Hedging density is shown as unstable rather than assigned a primary PCS value.
Figure 6: Hindi-Urdu baseline comparison across all 12 features. Differences are small overall, but this figure should not be interpreted as a formal equivalence test.
Table 8: Uncertainty Avoidance (UAI) feature scoring anchors. Higher scores indicate more uncertainty-avoiding communication.

Table 9: Stable-only PCS by model and cultural dimension. UAI rows exclude the unstable hedging-density ratios for all models, and Ministral’s PDI mean excludes one unstable communication-channel cell.
Table 10: Stable-only PCS by model and language. Most rows summarize 11 stable features; MinistralNepali summarizes 10 because two cells are unstable.
Table 7: Individualism-Collectivism (IDV) feature scoring anchors. Higher scores indicate more collectivist framing.
Table 6: Power Distance (PDI) feature scoring anchors. Higher scores indicate more hierarchical, face-conscious communication.
Table 1: Conceptual interpretation of the three pragmatic dimensions used in the study. We use them as operational axes for scoring generated text, not as exhaustive descriptions of entire cultures.

Table 16: Feature-level implicit sensitivity ranked by median stable PCS across languages. Hedging_density is listed as unstable rather than ranked numerically because its explicit gap is negative in all five languages.

Table 15: Representative response excerpts from English Gemini-3-flash (sample 1). The PDI example shows partial recovery of the hierarchy-sensitive strategy under implicit cueing, while the IDV example shows that Prompt C moves only partway toward the collectivist behavior seen in Prompt B.

Figure 5: Language Default Index (LDI) heatmap across all 12 features and five languages. Cell values are Prompt A means on the 1–7 scale, and darker cells indicate higher baseline scores. Rows are grouped by dimension.

Table 14: Feature-level Hindi-Urdu divergence. Largest divergence (expert_deference, HUD = 0.150) remains small in absolute terms. Primary paired test on matched scenario-feature means: t = 0.96, p = 0.339, dz = 0.06.

Table 12: Language Default Index (LDI) scores for all language-feature combinations. Values are mean ± SD. Higher IDV scores indicate more collectivist defaults; higher PDI scores indicate more hierarchical defaults; higher UAI scores indicate more uncertainty-avoiding defaults.
